content.text: is a systematic software framework for measuring how well an LLM-backed agent performs across a defined set of tasks. Where unit tests verify that individual functions behave correctly, an eval harness exercises the whole agent — prompting it, executing its tool calls against controlled stubs, recording every turn, and scoring the results against ground-truth expectations.

The harness sits between the raw agent code and the CI/CD pipeline. At its core it answers five questions for every scenario run: Did the agent complete the task? Did it call the right tools in the right order? Did it fabricate information not supported by context? How long did each scenario take end-to-end? And how do these numbers compare to the last approved baseline?

A well-designed harness treats evaluation as a first-class engineering concern, not an afterthought. It version-controls its scenarios alongside the code they test, runs deterministically in any environment, and integrates with the same continuous integration pipelines used for conventional software. The result is a repeatable, comparable metric stream that tracks agent quality across model upgrades, prompt edits, and architectural changes.

LLM-backed agents are non-deterministic. Two invocations of the same agent with the same input can produce different tool sequences, different intermediate reasoning, and different final text. This non-determinism makes traditional assertion-based testing brittle — a hard-coded expected string will fail on the next model update even when the agent's behaviour is correct. An evaluation harness responds to this reality by measuring
across many runs rather than binary pass/fail on exact output.

Procurement and model-selection decisions depend on trustworthy capability data. When comparing a GPT-4o checkpoint against Claude 3.5 Sonnet on a specific task distribution, a reproducible harness produces numbers that generalise beyond the demo scenario. Without it, model comparisons rest on anecdotal impressions that do not survive deployment conditions.

Regression risk is the other pressing concern. Every prompt change, every tool schema update, every model version bump is a potential capability regression. A harness that runs automatically on every pull request catches regressions before they reach production, turning an otherwise invisible quality decay into a blocked CI job.
— the same scenario set run against two model versions yields directly comparable numbers, enabling evidence-based model selection.
— automated gate in CI/CD prevents capability drops from reaching staging or production.
— mock tool layers eliminate the need for live API calls during most eval runs, reducing both latency and spend.
— per-scenario traces record every message and tool call, making it straightforward to understand exactly where and why a scenario failed.
— a scenario library organised by skill (retrieval, multi-step planning, code execution, form-filling) ensures that narrow benchmark gaming does not hide gaps in adjacent capabilities.
— aggregate dashboards translate raw eval numbers into business-meaningful KPIs that non-engineers can interpret and act on.

Evaluation harnesses are not free. They introduce meaningful engineering overhead: scenarios must be authored and maintained, tool mocks must be kept in sync with real tool schemas, and the harness infrastructure itself must be tested. Teams that skip this investment pay later through undetected regressions and guesswork-driven model procurement.
— a harness only measures what its scenarios cover. Novel failure modes outside the scenario library are invisible until a production incident surfaces them.
— tool simulators that fall out of sync with real tool behaviour produce eval scores that do not reflect production performance.

— running hundreds of scenarios against a large model for every PR can add significant cost and latency to the CI pipeline. Tiered evaluation strategies (quick smoke tests on every PR, full suite nightly) mitigate this.
— writing high-quality expected tool calls and output patterns for complex tasks requires domain expertise. Poorly authored ground truth yields misleading metrics.
— fully mocked evaluations are reproducible but may miss failure modes that only emerge with live, unpredictable tool responses.
The harness operates as an orchestration layer that wraps the agent under test. Execution follows a fixed pipeline:

— the test runner reads all registered scenarios from the scenario library. Each scenario specifies an initial task description, the sequence of tool calls the agent is expected to make, and a pattern against which the final response will be matched.
— the agent is instantiated in an isolated context. All outbound tool calls are intercepted by the tool mock layer, which returns pre-scripted responses rather than hitting live endpoints.
— the test runner drives the agent through one full task cycle. Every message sent and received, every tool call made, and every intermediate reasoning step is written to a structured trace log.
— the metrics engine processes each trace, computing task completion, tool-call accuracy, hallucination rate, latency, and planning quality scores.
— aggregate metrics are assembled into an
and compared against a stored baseline. If any metric regresses beyond its allowed tolerance, the harness exits with a non-zero status code.

Scenarios are executed in parallel using asyncio, which keeps wall-clock time acceptable even for large scenario suites. Each scenario runs in its own isolated coroutine so that shared state corruption is impossible.

attrs.alt: Agent Capability Evaluation Harness architecture diagram showing the pipeline from Test Scenario Library through Test Runner, Agent Under Test, Tool Simulator, Result Collector, Metrics Engine, and Report Generator, with key metric targets displayed

content.text: The pipeline flows left-to-right through six stages. The
is a versioned store of scenario definitions. The
provides a controlled execution environment with no external side effects. The
intercepts all tool calls and returns deterministic responses. The
stores the full per-scenario trace. Finally, the

The regression gate at the bottom of the diagram compares every run's aggregate metrics against a committed baseline snapshot. A metric that drops by more than its configured tolerance fails the build, ensuring that no model update or prompt change silently degrades performance.
An evaluation harness is appropriate whenever an LLM agent is being deployed to perform consequential tasks. Specific decision triggers include:
You are comparing two or more LLM models or versions before committing to a procurement decision.
The agent's tool-use behaviour must meet a minimum accuracy threshold for business or compliance reasons (e.g. a procurement agent that calls ERP APIs must not hallucinate purchase order numbers).
The system prompt or tool schemas are being actively iterated and regressions must be detected automatically.
Multiple teams contribute to the agent's configuration and an objective scoring mechanism is needed to resolve disagreements.
Latency SLOs exist and must be tracked across agent versions.
The organisation must demonstrate due diligence on AI quality to auditors or regulators.

For exploratory prototypes and proof-of-concept work where requirements are not yet stable, a full harness is premature. A lightweight scenario notebook or manual spot-check is sufficient until the agent's task definition is locked.
The following implementation covers the core harness infrastructure: scenario registration, parallel execution, and metric aggregation. The

from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import re
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable, Awaitable


@dataclass
class Scenario:
    scenario_id: str
    task: str
    expected_tools: list[str]           # ordered list of expected tool names
    expected_output_pattern: str        # regex applied to final response
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)


@dataclass
class ScenarioResult:
    scenario_id: str
    task_complete: bool
    tool_calls_made: list[str]
    final_response: str
    latency_ms: float
    hallucination_count: int = 0


@dataclass
class EvalReport:
# ... (truncated)
The following two classes handle the two most nuanced metric computations: tool-use accuracy and hallucination detection.

Tool-use accuracy is computed as precision, recall, and F1 against an expected set of tool names. Parallel tool use — where the model emits multiple tool calls in a single turn — is handled by treating all calls within a single turn as a bag and computing set-level overlap.

uses lightweight keyword-matching against a set of ground-truth facts. In production this would be replaced with an LLM-as-judge call or an NLI model, but a fast lexical check catches the most blatant violations during CI without requiring an additional model call.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import re


@dataclass
class HallucinationSpan:
    text: str
    confidence: float   # 0.0 = uncertain, 1.0 = confirmed conflict
    reason: str


class HallucinationDetector:
    """Keyword-based hallucination check against ground-truth facts.

    This is a first-pass filter. Any span flagged here should be reviewed
    by an LLM judge before being counted as a confirmed hallucination.
    """

    # Patterns that often accompany fabricated details
    _HEDGE_PREFIXES = re.compile(
        r"\b(as of|according to|in \d{4}|the latest|recently|studies show)",
        re.I,
    )

    def check(
        self,
        response_text: str,
        ground_truth_facts: list[str],
    ) -> list[HallucinationSpan]:
        """Return spans in response_text that conflict with ground_truth_facts.
# ... (truncated)

Consider a procurement agent that handles supplier quote requests. Its task distribution includes: fetching a supplier catalogue item by SKU, comparing three competing quotes by unit price, confirming a purchase order number exists in the ERP, and drafting an approval email. The eval harness for this agent registers the following scenarios:
Compare quotes from vendors A, B, C
In a model upgrade from an earlier checkpoint to a newer one, the harness revealed that
regressed: the newer model sometimes called

, dropping tool F1 from 1.0 to 0.5. The CI gate blocked the upgrade until the system prompt was updated to reinforce the correct tool name. Without the harness, this regression would have reached production.

— an agent that scores 100% on scenarios where all tools return valid data may still fail catastrophically when a tool returns an error or empty result. Always include adversarial and error-injection scenarios.
Using exact string matching for pass/fail
— exact match is too brittle for LLM output. Use regex patterns or semantic similarity scores that tolerate natural variation in phrasing.
Conflating the eval set with the training or fine-tuning set
— scenarios used to improve the agent (through prompt iteration or fine-tuning) must not be reused unchanged as evaluation scenarios. Contaminated eval suites produce inflated scores.
— evaluating only the final response misses failures in intermediate reasoning. Track whether the agent's tool calls on turn 3 correctly reference information retrieved on turn 1.
— high temperature increases result variance and can make regressions appear as noise. Use temperature=0 for deterministic comparisons when the model and API support it.
Treating latency as a secondary metric
— a more capable model that doubles average latency may not be a net improvement for real-time use cases. Latency must be a gated metric, not a footnote.
directory committed to the same repository as the agent. Scenario IDs are stable references in PR comments, Jira tickets, and incident reports.
to generate per-capability breakdowns in the report. A regression that appears only in
scenarios is far easier to diagnose than a 2% drop in overall task completion.
— run a fast smoke subset (10–20 critical scenarios) on every PR. Run the full suite nightly or before releases. This balances coverage with pipeline speed.

as a JSON file in the repository. The CI script loads this file and computes per-metric delta rather than comparing against a hardcoded threshold, allowing the bar to rise automatically as the agent improves.
Log full traces, not just aggregates
— aggregate metrics identify that a regression exists; per-scenario traces identify where. Store traces for at least the last N runs so that bisection is possible.
Decouple the harness from the agent implementation
— the harness should accept any callable that matches the
signature. This makes it possible to evaluate different agent architectures (ReAct, plan-and-execute, function-calling) against the same scenario suite.
At small scale (under 50 scenarios), a fully sequential run is acceptable. As the scenario library grows to hundreds of entries, parallel execution becomes necessary. The
approach in the harness above saturates I/O concurrency but does not parallelize CPU-bound metric computation. For suites exceeding 500 scenarios, consider the following:
) to cap the number of inflight LLM requests. Uncapped concurrency exceeds rate limits and inflates latency measurements.

— hash each scenario definition and cache its result. Only re-run scenarios whose hash changed since the last run. This dramatically reduces cost on iterative prompt edits that affect only a subset of scenarios.
— if LLM-as-judge calls are used for hallucination detection, each judge call is itself an LLM invocation. Run these in a separate
to avoid contention with the primary asyncio event loop.

— instrument the harness to record token counts per scenario using the model API's usage fields. Aggregate cost becomes a tracked metric alongside capability metrics, preventing unchecked eval spending.

— for scenarios that share a long system prompt, use the model provider's prompt caching feature (e.g., Anthropic's cache control breakpoints). This reduces both latency and per-scenario cost on repeated runs.
Several purpose-built evaluation frameworks exist alongside hand-rolled harnesses. Understanding the trade-offs prevents reinventing infrastructure that is already available.
Safety and capability benchmarking with built-in solvers and scorers
Teams already on LangChain; integrated tracing and dataset management
LLM-as-judge scoring with UI diff views between runs
Faithfulness, answer relevancy, and context recall for retrieval-augmented agents
Lower-level tool mock construction and assertion; composable with any harness

Hand-rolling a harness using the patterns in this document is appropriate when: the agent framework is bespoke, the scenario format needs tight integration with an internal data catalogue, or external SaaS evaluation tooling is not approved by the organisation's security review.
Evaluation harnesses that call live LLM APIs introduce several security and reliability considerations that are easy to overlook.
— the harness should use a dedicated API key scoped to evaluation, separate from the production key. Rate limit spikes from a large eval run should not affect the production quota.

— if scenarios are derived from real business data (real supplier names, actual PO numbers), they must be anonymised or replaced with synthetic equivalents before being committed to version control. Real data in scenarios is both a security risk and a compliance violation in regulated industries.

— mock tools that return only successful responses make the agent appear more robust than it is. Include mock responses that simulate timeouts, malformed JSON, rate limit errors (HTTP 429), and missing resource errors (HTTP 404). A harness that only ever succeeds is not measuring production readiness.

— full conversation traces may contain sensitive content generated by the LLM. Store them in access-controlled locations, apply retention policies, and avoid logging traces to public CI artifact stores.

— the baseline snapshot committed to the repository must be generated using the same harness configuration (model parameters, mock responses, temperature) as subsequent runs. A baseline generated under different conditions produces spurious regression signals.
Think of an agent capability evaluation harness as a
flight simulator for an AI pilot

. The flight simulator reproduces cockpit instruments, weather conditions, and air traffic control communications — none of it is real, but it is sufficiently faithful to reveal whether the pilot handles turbulence, engine failure, and navigation errors correctly. The evaluation harness does the same for an agent: it constructs a controlled environment that reproduces the task conditions the agent will encounter in production, intercepts every "control input" (tool call) the agent makes, and records how the flight ends (task outcome).

Just as pilots are certified against a defined scenario catalogue before being cleared for live flights, agents should be measured against a defined scenario library before being deployed to handle real business processes. The scenarios that appear in the harness's library are the agent's certification syllabus. The metrics it must meet are the pass marks.

The analogy extends to maintenance: after every significant change to the aircraft (model upgrade) or training procedures (prompt change), the pilot re-sits specific simulator checks — not the entire certification. Tiered evaluation in CI mirrors this exactly: targeted smoke tests on every change, full recertification before major releases.

An eval harness wraps the agent in a controlled environment, drives it through a library of scenarios, and measures task completion, tool-use accuracy, hallucination rate, and latency — all automatically and reproducibly.
Tool-use accuracy is best expressed as precision, recall, and F1 against an expected tool-call set; order-sensitive evaluation requires a sequence-alignment metric such as LCS similarity.
Mock tool layers are essential for determinism, cost control, and error-injection testing; they must be kept in sync with real tool schemas to avoid misleading eval scores.
The CI regression gate compares aggregate metrics against a committed baseline snapshot and fails the build on any metric that drops beyond its configured tolerance.
Tiered evaluation — fast smoke tests on every PR, full suite nightly — balances coverage against CI pipeline cost and latency.
Scenarios must cover adversarial and error-injection cases, not only happy-path flows; otherwise the harness measures best-case performance rather than production readiness.
Tagging scenarios by capability area (retrieval, multi-step planning, tool use) enables per-capability regression reports that dramatically reduce debugging time compared to aggregate-only metrics.
